questions.stem: A 2-year-old boy presents with recurrent bruising and swelling of his right knee after a minor fall. He has no significant past medical history. His mother reports that he bruises easily and his uncle had a bleeding disorder. On examination, there is a large knee effusion with restricted movement. Blood tests show normal platelet count, prolonged aPTT, normal PT, and decreased factor VIII activity. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The prolonged aPTT with normal PT and decreased factor VIII activity is classic for haemophilia A. Haemophilia B (factor IX deficiency) would show decreased factor IX. Von Willebrand disease typically affects both bleeding time and aPTT, and factor VIII may be low but von Willebrand factor antigen/activity is usually decreased. Factor XI deficiency is autosomal and not X-linked like this family history suggests. Platelet disorders usually have normal aPTT.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man presents with prolonged bleeding after dental extraction. He has no previous bleeding history and no family history of bleeding disorders. His full blood count and coagulation screen show: Hb 140 g/L, platelets 280 x10^9/L, PT 12 s (normal 10-14), aPTT 42 s (normal 25-35). Mixing study shows correction with normal plasma. Factor VIII assay is 25 IU/dL (normal 50-150). What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Mild haemophilia A often presents later in life with bleeding after surgery or trauma. Factor VIII activity of 25 IU/dL is consistent with mild deficiency. Acquired haemophilia A typically has an inhibitor that does not correct on mixing; this patient's mixing study corrected. Von Willebrand disease type 1 usually has a prolonged bleeding time or PFA-100, and von Willebrand factor levels would be low. Factor XI deficiency is autosomal and aPTT prolongation may be less with normal factor VIII. Vitamin K deficiency would prolong PT more than aPTT.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man with known severe haemophilia A (factor VIII <1% on prophylaxis) presents with acute onset of severe right groin and anterior thigh pain, inability to straighten the hip, and paraesthesia over the anteromedial thigh. He has had similar episodes before. On examination, the right hip is held in flexion and external rotation, and there is weakness of hip flexion. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Septic arthritis of the right hip
Acute haemarthrosis of the right hip
Iliopsoas bleed with femoral nerve compression
Acute compartment syndrome of the thigh
questions.explanation: Spontaneous iliopsoas bleed is a classic complication in severe haemophilia A, causing femoral nerve compression with pain in groin/thigh, hip flexion deformity, and sensory loss in anteromedial thigh. Acute haemarthrosis of the hip would cause more diffuse pain and restricted range but not typically femoral nerve signs. Septic arthritis would be hot, red, and systemically unwell. Stress fracture is unlikely spontaneous. Compartment syndrome would involve tense swelling and more diffuse neurological signs.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with severe haemophilia A on regular prophylaxis with factor VIII concentrate presents with a sudden increase in bleeding frequency and severity. He now bleeds into joints without trauma and does not respond to standard doses of factor VIII. Laboratory results: aPTT prolonged, mixing study does not correct, factor VIII assay <1%, and Bethesda assay shows 5 Bethesda units. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The development of an alloantibody (inhibitor) to factor VIII is a known complication in haemophilia A, manifesting as breakthrough bleeding despite appropriate factor replacement. The non-correcting mixing study and measurable Bethesda units confirm the inhibitor. Acquired haemophilia A occurs in older adults without prior haemophilia and is an autoantibody, not an alloantibody in a known haemophiliac. Lupus anticoagulant would also not correct but is not associated with factor VIII deficiency. Factor IX inhibitor is incorrect as the patient has haemophilia A. NSAID-induced platelet dysfunction should not affect factor VIII levels.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with menorrhagia and easy bruising. She has no significant past medical history. She takes no medications. Her father and paternal uncle have mild haemophilia A. Laboratory investigations show: Hb 120 g/L, platelets 350 x10^9/L, PT 11 s, aPTT 38 s, bleeding time normal, von Willebrand factor antigen and activity normal. Factor VIII level is 45 IU/dL (normal 50-150). What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: This woman is an obligate carrier (father affected) and has mild bleeding symptoms with borderline low factor VIII, consistent with a symptomatic carrier due to skewed X-inactivation. True mild haemophilia A in a female is rare but possible with homozygous mutation or Turner syndrome; however, carrier is more likely given family history. Von Willebrand disease type 2N (Normandy) causes low factor VIII but normal vWF; however, it is autosomal recessive and typically not inherited from an affected father alone. Factor XI deficiency would cause prolonged aPTT with normal factor VIII. Dysfibrinogenaemia affects thrombin time and fibrinogen.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old boy presents with easy bruising and a haemarthrosis after a minor fall. Coagulation screen shows a prolonged activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT) but normal prothrombin time (PT) and platelet count. What is the most appropriate next investigation to characterise the aPTT prolongation?
options: Perform a mixing study with normal plasma
questions.explanation: A prolonged aPTT with normal PT and platelets suggests a deficiency in the intrinsic pathway. A mixing study (mixing patient plasma with normal pooled plasma) distinguishes between a factor deficiency (correction to normal) and an inhibitor (partial or no correction). This is the essential next step before specific factor assays.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with mild bleeding (epistaxis, easy bruising) is found to have a prolonged aPTT. Factor VIII activity is borderline low at 30 IU/dL (normal 50-150). What is the single most appropriate next test to help confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: A low factor VIII level with mild bleeding raises the possibility of type 2N von Willebrand disease (VWD), which causes decreased factor VIII binding and low plasma levels. Measuring von Willebrand factor antigen (vWF:Ag) helps differentiate VWD from haemophilia A – a normal vWF:Ag suggests haemophilia A, while low vWF:Ag suggests VWD. Thus, vWF:Ag is the critical next step.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man presents with acute right knee haemarthrosis after a basketball game. Coagulation screen shows aPTT 75 seconds (normal 26-36) with normal PT. He has no prior bleeding history but his brother has severe haemophilia A. What is the most appropriate single investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: In a patient with a family history of haemophilia A, acute haemarthrosis, and isolated aPTT prolongation, the definitive diagnostic test is factor VIII activity assay. This confirms the diagnosis and quantifies severity (severe <1%, moderate 1-5%, mild >5%). This is the 'never-miss' test for haemophilia A.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old known haemophilia A patient (factor VIII activity 3%) has developed recurrent joint bleeds despite adequate factor VIII replacement. His aPTT is prolonged and does not correct on mixing study. What is the single most appropriate test to confirm the underlying problem?
options: Perform a Bethesda assay for factor VIII inhibitor
questions.explanation: A non-correcting mixing study in a haemophilia A patient with loss of response to factor VIII replacement strongly suggests development of an alloantibody inhibitor. The Bethesda assay quantifies the inhibitor titre and is the confirmatory test. Factor VIII activity alone would be low and does not explain the failure of correction.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with no bleeding history is referred due to prolonged aPTT (45 seconds) found on routine screening. Her father and brother have haemophilia A. She wants to know if she is a carrier. Which investigation is most appropriate?
questions.explanation: For carrier detection in haemophilia A, direct mutation analysis (factor VIII gene sequencing) is the gold standard because factor VIII levels may be normal due to lyonisation. Genetic testing provides definitive carrier status. Factor VIII activity is a screening test but cannot exclude carrier status if normal.

questions.stem: A 20-year-old man with known mild haemophilia A (baseline factor VIII 8 IU/dL) presents with a swollen, painful knee after a minor twist. There is no evidence of neurovascular compromise. He has previously had a good response to desmopressin. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Tranexamic acid 1 g orally three times daily
questions.explanation: Desmopressin is first-line for mild-to-moderate haemophilia A without inhibitors if the patient is a known responder, as it releases endogenous factor VIII. Factor VIII concentrate is reserved for severe deficiency or if desmopressin fails.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with severe haemophilia A (factor VIII <1 IU/dL) is brought to the emergency department after a road traffic accident. He has a large expanding haematoma in the thigh with suspected retroperitoneal bleeding. What is the most appropriate immediate therapy?
questions.explanation: In severe haemophilia A with a major bleed, immediate factor VIII replacement is essential. Recombinant factor VIII concentrate is first-line; desmopressin is ineffective in severe deficiency. Cryoprecipitate and FFP are not recommended due to viral risk and lower potency.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man with severe haemophilia A and a high-titre factor VIII inhibitor (10 Bethesda units) presents with sudden severe headache, vomiting, and confusion. CT scan shows an intracranial haemorrhage. What is the most appropriate first-line haemostatic therapy?
questions.explanation: With high-titre inhibitors, factor VIII is ineffective. Recombinant factor VIIa (or activated prothrombin complex concentrate) is a bypassing agent that promotes thrombin generation and is first-line for life-threatening bleeds in this setting.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old boy with severe haemophilia A (factor VIII <1 IU/dL) is seen in the haematology clinic. He has had several haemarthroses and one muscle bleed. His parents ask about a treatment to prevent future bleeds. What is the most appropriate management?
options: On-demand recombinant factor VIII only for bleeds
Regular recombinant factor VIII infusions three times weekly (prophylaxis)
questions.explanation: Prophylaxis with factor VIII concentrate is recommended for children with severe haemophilia to prevent joint damage and reduce bleed frequency. Desmopressin is ineffective in severe deficiency, and on-demand therapy is acceptable but not preventive.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with mild haemophilia A (baseline factor VIII 12 IU/dL) is scheduled for dental extraction of a lower molar. He has no inhibitors and has previously responded to desmopressin. He asks about medication to reduce bleeding risk. Which of the following is contraindicated?
options: Aspirin 75 mg once daily for dental pain
Recombinant factor VIII 20 IU/kg intravenously prior to extraction
Desmopressin 0.3 mcg/kg intravenously before the procedure
questions.explanation: Aspirin irreversibly inhibits platelet cyclooxygenase and impairs platelet function, increasing bleeding risk in haemophilia. Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs are generally avoided. Desmopressin, tranexamic acid, and factor VIII are all used for perioperative haemostasis.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old boy presents with a swollen, warm left knee after a minor fall. No known bleeding disorder. Examination reveals a tense effusion. Bruising elsewhere. INR normal, aPTT prolonged. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Order a coagulation screen including fibrinogen
Arrange an urgent orthopaedic review for aspiration
questions.explanation: A prolonged aPTT with normal INR suggests a factor deficiency. Measuring factor VIII activity is the immediate next step to confirm haemophilia A and assess severity. A mixing study could distinguish deficiency from inhibitor but is not the first test. Aspiration is contraindicated without factor replacement.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with known mild haemophilia A (baseline factor VIII 15 IU/dL) presents for a dental extraction. He has tolerated previous minor procedures with desmopressin. What is the single most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Check factor VIII level and response to desmopressin
Administer factor VIII concentrate 50 IU/kg intravenously
Schedule a haematology review for next week
questions.explanation: For mild haemophilia A undergoing a minor procedure, desmopressin is first-line to raise factor VIII. The patient is a known responder. Tranexamic acid alone is insufficient; factor VIII concentrate is reserved for non-responders or severe disease. Checking level is not the immediate step; treatment should precede.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old male with severe haemophilia A (factor VIII <1%) was found unconscious after a fall. CT head shows an extradural haemorrhage. He has no known inhibitors. His last factor VIII infusion was 3 days ago. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Give a bolus of factor VIII concentrate 50 IU/kg IV
Administer recombinant factor VIIa 90 mcg/kg IV
questions.explanation: In life-threatening bleeding of severe haemophilia A, immediate factor VIII replacement is the priority to achieve haemostasis. Neurosurgery may be needed but must wait for factor coverage. rFVIIa is for inhibitor patients; tranexamic acid is an adjunct, not primary. Inhibitor screen is not urgent before treatment.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with severe haemophilia A presents with a haemarthrosis that does not improve despite two doses of standard factor VIII concentrate. Previous infusions have been effective. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Administer activated prothrombin complex concentrate (aPCC) 75 IU/kg
Perform a Bethesda assay to test for inhibitors
Arrange for surgical drainage of the joint
Increase the dose of factor VIII concentrate to 50 IU/kg
questions.explanation: Lack of response to standard factor VIII in a previously responsive patient suggests an inhibitor. The immediate next step is to confirm this with a Bethesda assay. Bypassing agents (aPCC) are considered only after inhibitor confirmation. Increasing the dose may be ineffective; steroids and drainage are not indicated solely for suspected inhibitor.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with severe haemophilia A is scheduled for elective total knee replacement. He has no inhibitors. What is the single most appropriate immediate preoperative action?
options: Check a baseline factor VIII level and inhibitor screen
Give desmopressin 0.3 mcg/kg to assess response
Administer a bolus of factor VIII concentrate 50 IU/kg
Start continuous infusion of factor VIII
Refer to a haemophilia treatment centre for perioperative planning
questions.explanation: For major surgery in severe haemophilia A, immediate preoperative factor VIII replacement is necessary to achieve target levels (e.g., 80-100 IU/dL). Checking levels is important but does not replace administration. Desmopressin is ineffective in severe disease. Continuous infusion is an option but not the immediate next step; a bolus is given first.

questions.stem: A 9-month-old boy presents with a large scalp haematoma after a minor bump. He has not been previously diagnosed with a bleeding disorder. On examination, you note a tense, bulging anterior fontanelle. Which finding most urgently suggests a life-threatening bleed?
questions.explanation: A bulging anterior fontanelle in an infant indicates raised intracranial pressure, which in this context suggests intracranial haemorrhage — a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate imaging and factor replacement. Other findings like haemarthrosis or prolonged aPTT are important but not as acutely dangerous.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man with severe haemophilia A (factor VIII <1 IU/dL) presents with acute-onset severe left groin and thigh pain. He is unable to extend his left hip and is tachycardic. Which diagnosis must be considered as the cause of his symptoms?
questions.explanation: Iliopsoas haematoma is a classic complication of haemophilia, presenting with groin pain, hip held in flexion, and inability to extend the hip. It can cause life-threatening blood loss and femoral nerve compression, requiring urgent factor replacement.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with mild haemophilia A (factor VIII 15 IU/dL) presents with an acutely painful, swollen, and erythematous right knee. He is apyrexial and has no trauma. Which diagnosis must be urgently excluded?
questions.explanation: In any patient presenting with an acute monoarthritis, septic arthritis must be ruled out as it requires urgent antibiotics and joint drainage. Although haemarthrosis is more common in haemophilia, the presence of erythema and warmth raises concern for infection.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old boy with severe haemophilia A on regular prophylactic factor VIII presents with a painful, swollen left elbow after a fall. He received his usual dose of factor VIII (50 IU/kg) three hours ago, but the swelling and pain have not improved. Which complication is the most likely cause?
questions.explanation: Failure of factor VIII to control a bleeding episode, despite adequate dosing, is a red flag for inhibitor development. Inhibitory antibodies neutralise factor VIII, rendering standard replacement less effective and significantly increasing bleeding risk.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old boy with severe haemophilia A (factor VIII <1 IU/dL) presents after falling from a tricycle. He initially appeared well but two hours later has become drowsy and vomited once. Which clinical feature is most indicative of intracranial haemorrhage?
questions.explanation: Altered consciousness, such as drowsiness, is a key sign of raised intracranial pressure and potential intracranial haemorrhage in a haemophiliac with head trauma. Although vomiting and headache are also concerning, drowsiness is a more direct indicator of neurological compromise.
